theorems.title: Optimal majority-vote weighting
theorems.statementHtml:
For any probability space \(\langle X,\Sigma,V\rangle\) and any \(\epsilon,\gamma>0\), if the weightor plays the majority-vote game for \(k\) iterations, where \(k\) satisfies

\[\sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor}\binom{k}{j}\left(\frac12+\gamma\right)^j\left(\frac12-\gamma\right)^{k-j}\leq\epsilon,\tag{2}\]

and uses the following weighting in iteration \(i\)

For any set \(A\) in the \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\Sigma\)

\[W(A)=\sum_{r=0}^{i}V(A\cap X_r^i)\alpha_r^i/Z_i,\tag{3}\]

where \(Z_i=\sum_{r=0}^{i}V(X_r^i)\alpha_r^i\), then the reward at the end of the game is at least \(1-\epsilon\), independent of the strategy used by the chooser.
theorems.proofHtml:
From Equation (6) it is immediate that the left hand side of Equation (2) is equal to \(\beta_0^0\); thus, by choice of \(k\), \(\beta_0^0\leq\epsilon\), which means that the initial expected potential is small. Combining this with the inequality from Lemma 2.2, which implies that the potential never increases, we get

\[\epsilon\geq\beta_0^0\geq\sum_{r=0}^{k}q_r^k\beta_r^k.\]

On the other hand, from Equation (4) we have

\[\sum_{r=0}^{k}q_r^k\beta_r^k=\sum_{r=0}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor}q_r^k=V(L).\]

Thus the value of the loss set \(L\) is at most \(\epsilon\).

theorems.title: Average potential never increases
theorems.statementHtml:
If the weighting scheme described in Equation (3) is used by the weightor, then

\[\beta_0^0\geq\sum_{r=0}^{1}q_r^1\beta_r^1\geq\sum_{r=0}^{2}q_r^2\beta_r^2\geq\cdots\geq\sum_{r=0}^{k}q_r^k\beta_r^k,\]

for any strategy of the chooser.
theorems.proofHtml:
Recall that \(q_r^i=V(X_r^i)\) and \(x_r^i=V(M_r^i)/V(X_r^i)\). At each iteration \(i\) the adversary chooses the variables \(0\leq x_r^i\leq1\), and we get the following formula for the transition to the next iteration:

\[q_r^{i+1}=q_{r-1}^{i}x_{r-1}^{i}+q_r^i(1-x_r^i)\quad\text{for }1\leq r\leq i,\tag{8}\]

\[q_0^{i+1}=q_0^i(1-x_0^i),\qquad q_{i+1}^{i+1}=q_i^ix_i^i.\]

Using this we can get a formula that relates the sum \(\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\beta_r^i\) for consecutive iterations. Rearranging the transition sum gives

\[\sum_{r=0}^{i+1}q_r^{i+1}\beta_r^{i+1}=\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\beta_r^i+\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^ix_r^i(\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}-\beta_r^{i+1}).\tag{9}\]

On the other hand, from the weight restriction we get \(\sum_{r=0}^{i}W(M_r^i)\geq\frac12+\gamma\), and, as \(M_r^i\subseteq X_r^i\), the definition of the weight function gives

\[\frac{1}{Z_i}\sum_{r=0}^{i}V(M_r^i)\alpha_r^i\geq\frac12+\gamma.\tag{11}\]

Using the definitions of \(q_r^i,x_r^i,Z_i\), this can be written as

\[\frac{\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^ix_r^i\alpha_r^i}{\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\alpha_r^i}\geq\frac12+\gamma.\tag{12}\]

Using Equation (7) and the fact that \(\beta_r^{i+1}>\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}\), we get

\[\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^ix_r^i(\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}-\beta_r^{i+1})\leq\left(\frac12+\gamma\right)\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i(\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}-\beta_r^{i+1}).\tag{13}\]

Substituting (13) into the right hand side of (9), and using Equation (5), finally gives

\[\sum_{r=0}^{i+1}q_r^{i+1}\beta_r^{i+1}\leq\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\left[\left(\frac12+\gamma\right)\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}+\left(\frac12-\gamma\right)\beta_r^{i+1}\right]=\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\beta_r^i.\]

theorems.title: Explicit iteration bound
theorems.statementHtml:
Theorem 2.1 holds if the number of iterations satisfies

\[k\geq\frac{1}{2\gamma^2}\ln\frac{1}{2\epsilon}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Suppose that a biased coin, whose probability for heads is \(1/2+\gamma\), is tossed \(k\) times. The left hand side of Inequality (2) describes the probability that at most half of the coin tosses come up heads. We can upper bound the probability of this event as follows. The number of sequences of \(k\) coin tosses that have at least as many heads as tails is at most \(2^{k-1}\). The probability of any single sequence is at most \([(1/2-\gamma)(1/2+\gamma)]^{k/2}\). Thus we get that a sufficient condition on \(k\) for Inequality (2) to hold is

\[2^{k-1}[(1/2-\gamma)(1/2+\gamma)]^{k/2}\leq\epsilon.\]

Reordering this inequality, we get that it is equivalent to

\[k/2\geq\frac{\ln(1/2\epsilon)}{-\ln(1-4\gamma^2)}.\]

Using the fact that \(\ln(1-x)\leq-x\) for \(0\leq x<1\), we find that the condition on \(k\) in the statement of the corollary is a sufficient condition for Inequality (2).

theorems.title: Optimal chooser strategy on divisible spaces
theorems.statementHtml:
If the probability space \(\langle X,\Sigma,V\rangle\) is divisible, then there exists a strategy for the chooser such that for any \(k\) and \(\gamma\), the loss is at least

\[\sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor}\binom{k}{j}\left(\frac12+\gamma\right)^j\left(\frac12-\gamma\right)^{k-j}.\tag{14}\]

This means that the choice of \(k\) in Equation (2) of Theorem 2.1 is the smallest possible.
theorems.proofHtml:
Let us denote the set of points in \(X\) that have been marked in a particular way by \(X_s\), where \(s=\langle s_1,s_2,\ldots,s_i\rangle\) is a binary vector of length \(i\leq k\), such that \(s_j\) is 1 if \(x\) has been marked on iteration \(j\) and 0 otherwise. The goal of the chooser is to mark points so that for each sequence of marks \(s\), the probability of \(X_s\) would be equal to the probability that \(s\) is generated by \(i\) random draws of a biased coin whose probability of heads is \(1/2+\gamma\).

The strategy is defined as follows. In the \(i\)th iteration, the space \(X\) is divided into the \(2^{i-1}\) sets \(X_s\) corresponding to the different binary sequences of length \(i-1\). From Lemma 2.5 we get that for each \(s\) there exists a set \(A_s\subseteq X_s\) in \(\Sigma\) such that \(V(A_s)=(1/2+\gamma)V(X_s)\), and \(W(A_s)\geq(1/2+\gamma)W(X_s)\). The chooser marks the points in the set \(\bigcup_s A_s\), which makes \(X_{s1}\) be \(A_s\) and \(X_{s0}\) be \(X_s\setminus A_s\). This is a legal marking because of the condition on \(W(A_s)\).

The loss set is

\[\bigcup\left\{X_s\mid s=\langle s_1,\ldots,s_k\rangle,\ \sum_{i=1}^{k}s_i\leq k/2\right\}.\]

It is easy to calculate the value of the loss set and show that it is equal to the loss defined in (14), which completes the proof.

theorems.title: A high-weight subset of prescribed value
theorems.statementHtml: Suppose that the probability space \(\langle X,\Sigma,V\rangle\) is divisible, and that \(W\) is another probability measure defined on \(\langle X,\Sigma\rangle\). Then for any set \(A\in\Sigma\) there exists a set \(A'\subseteq A\), \(A'\in\Sigma\), such that \(V(A')=(1/2+\gamma)V(A)\) and \(W(A')\geq(1/2+\gamma)W(A)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
First observe that as we are interested only in the ratio of the weight and value of \(G\) to that of \(D\), we can assume without loss of generality that \(V(D)=W(D)=1\).

Define the following series of partitions of \(D\): \(P_0=\{D\}\); \(P_1=\{D_0^1,D_1^1\}\), where the sets are disjoint and \(V(D_0^1)=V(D_1^1)=1/2\); and construct \(P_{i+1}\) from \(P_i\) by splitting each set in \(P_i\) into two disjoint equal-valued parts so that the value of each part is exactly \(2^{-i}\).

We shall now use the partitions \(P_0,P_1,P_2,\ldots\) to construct a series of sets \(G_0,G_1,G_2,\ldots\) that will provide better and better approximations of the target set \(G\). Assume that the binary expansion of \(1/2+\gamma\) is

\[\frac12+\gamma=\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}b_j2^{-j}\]

(note that \(b_0=0,b_1=1\)), and construct the sets \(G_i\) according to the following inductive procedure:

\[G_0=\varnothing,\qquad \Delta_i=\text{the set with the largest weight in }P_i\text{ that is not a subset of }G_i,\]

\[G_{i+1}=\begin{cases}G_i,&b_i=0,\\G_i\cup\Delta_i,&b_i=1.\end{cases}\]

It is clear that \(G_i\) is a monotonically increasing series of sets and that \(\lim_{i\to\infty}V(G_i)=1/2+\gamma\).

Note that all of the parts in \(P_i\) have equal value. Thus the ratio between the value of the complement of \(G_i\) and the value of \(\Delta_i\) is equal to the number of parts that are outside \(G_i\). On the other hand, the weight of \(\Delta_i\) is the largest among the parts outside \(G_i\). Thus the ratio of the weight of the complement of \(G_i\) to the weight of \(\Delta_i\) is at least the number of parts. This can be written as

\[\frac{W(\Delta_i)}{1-W(G_i)}\geq\frac{V(\Delta_i)}{1-V(G_i)}.\]

We shall now prove by induction on \(i\) that, for all \(i\geq0\), \(W(G_i)\geq V(G_i)\). For \(i=0\), \(G_0=\varnothing\), so the claim holds trivially. For \(i\geq1\), if \(b_i=0\), then \(G_{i+1}=G_i\), so the induction holds trivially. Otherwise, \(b_i=1\) and \(G_{i+1}=G_i\cup\Delta_i\), and we get

\[\begin{aligned}W(G_{i+1})&=W(G_i)+W(\Delta_i)\\&=V(G_i)+(W(G_i)-V(G_i))+W(\Delta_i)\\&\geq V(G_i)+(W(G_i)-V(G_i))+V(\Delta_i)\frac{1-W(G_i)}{1-V(G_i)}\\&=V(G_i)+V(\Delta_i)+(W(G_i)-V(G_i))\left[1-\frac{V(\Delta_i)}{1-V(G_i)}\right].\end{aligned}\]

The first two terms sum to \(V(G_{i+1})\), and the last term is positive because \(\Delta_i\subseteq\overline{G_i}\) implies that \(V(\Delta_i)\leq1-V(G_i)\), and because from the induction hypothesis \(W(G_i)-V(G_i)\geq0\). The induction hypothesis is thus proven.

Define \(G=\bigcup_{j=1}^{\infty}G_j\). As all \(\Delta_i\) are in the \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\Sigma\), so is \(G\). Also \(V(G)=\lim_{i\to\infty}V(G_i)=1/2+\gamma\). Similarly, \(W(G)=\lim_{i\to\infty}W(G_i)\geq1/2+\gamma\), which proves the lemma.
theorems.proofNote: The proof is deferred to Appendix C.

theorems.title: Majority representation from correlation
theorems.section: 2.2 The representational power of majority gates
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(f\) be a Boolean function over \(\{-1,1\}^n\) and \(H\) be a set of functions over the same domain. Then if \(k>2\ln(2)nD_H^{-2}(f)\), then \(f\) can be represented as

\[f(x)=\operatorname{MAJ}(h_1(x),\ldots,h_k(x)),\]

for some \(h_i\in H\).
theorems.proofHtml: Assume the majority-vote game is played over the domain \(\{-1,1\}^n\) and that the value of a set is the number of points in it divided by \(2^n\). Assume the chooser in the majority-vote game chooses which points to mark by selecting a function \(h\in H\) such that \(\Pr_D(h(x)\neq f(x))\leq1/2-\gamma\), and marking all \(x\) such that \(h(x)=f(x)\). By definition of \(D_H(f)\), such a function exists for every distribution \(D\) if \(\gamma=D_H(f)/2\). Theorem 2.1 provides us with a method for selecting the distributions \(D_i\), which correspond to the weightings \(W_i\). This selection guarantees that the majority over the corresponding hypotheses will be very close to \(f\). More specifically, it guarantees that if \(k=(1/2)\gamma^{-2}\ln(1/\epsilon)\), the number of points \(x\in\{-1,1\}^n\) such that \(\operatorname{MAJ}(h_1(x),\ldots,h_k(x))\neq f(x)\) is smaller than \(\epsilon2^n\). By setting \(\epsilon<2^{-n}\), we guarantee that \(\operatorname{MAJ}(h_1(x),\ldots,h_k(x))=f(x)\) for all \(x\in\{-1,1\}^n\). Plugging our selection for \(\gamma\) and \(\epsilon\) into \(k=(1/2)\gamma^{-2}\ln(1/\epsilon)\), we finish the proof.

theorems.title: Correlation from majority representation
theorems.section: 2.2 The representational power of majority gates
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(f\) and \(H\) be as in Theorem 2.6. Then if \(f\) can be represented as

\[f(x)=\operatorname{MAJ}(h_1(x),\ldots,h_k(x)),\]

where \(h_i\in H\) and \(k\) is odd, then \(D_H(f)\geq1/k\).
theorems.proofHtml:
From the definition of the majority function and the fact that \(k\) is odd, we get that for every \(x\in\{-1,1\}^n\), there are at least \((k+1)/2\) indices \(i\) such that \(h_i(x)=f(x)\). Fixing any distribution \(D\) over \(\{-1,1\}^n\), we get that

\[\sum_{i=1}^{k}\Pr_D(h_i(x)=f(x))=\sum_{x\in\{-1,1\}^n}\Pr_D\bigl(x:\lvert\{1\leq i\leq k\mid h_i(x)=f(x)\}\rvert\geq(k+1)/2\bigr).\]

The pigeon-hole principle guarantees that there exists at least one index \(1\leq i\leq k\) such that \(\Pr_D(h_i(x)=f(x))\geq(k+1)/(2k)\). This implies that \(D_H^D(f)\geq1/k\). As this holds for all \(D\), we get the statement of the lemma.

theorems.title: Consistency of BSamp
theorems.statementHtml: If all the hypotheses that are used by algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) are \(\epsilon_0\)-good, then the hypothesis \(h_M\), output by \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\), is consistent on the sample \(S\).
theorems.proofHtml: From the correspondence with the majority-vote game defined above, and from Theorem 2.1, we get that the error of the hypothesis output by \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) is smaller than \(1/m\). As the target distribution is uniform, it assigns each point in \(S\) probability \(1/m\). Thus the output hypothesis must be correct on all points in \(S\).

theorems.title: Sample bound for boosting by sub-sampling
theorems.statementHtml:
Let WeakLearn be a deterministic learning algorithm that generates, with probability \(\lambda>0\) over the random training examples with which it is trained, a deterministic hypothesis whose error is smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\), for some \(\gamma>0\). Assume the number of training examples required to achieve this is uniformly bounded by \(m_0\). Then the hypothesis \(h_M\) generated by \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) has the following property.

For any \(\epsilon,\delta>0\), if \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) uses a sample of size at least \(m\), where

\[m\geq\frac1\epsilon\left(\ln\frac{2}{\delta}+\frac{m_0}{2\gamma^2}(\ln m+1)^2\right),\]

then the probability that \(h_M\) has error larger than \(\epsilon\) is smaller than \(\delta\). Here the probability is defined over the random choice of the sample \(S\) and over the internal random coin flips in \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\).
theorems.proofHtml:
We are interested in bounding the probability of the set of samples and internal coin flips of \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) that generate a hypothesis that has error larger than \(\epsilon\). We do that by covering this set by two disjoint sets. The first set is the set of samples and coin flips that cause \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) to generate a hypothesis that is consistent with the sample and yet has error larger than \(\epsilon\). The second is the set of samples and coin flips that cause \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) to generate a hypothesis that is inconsistent with the sample. The first and second parts of the proof bound the probabilities of these two sets respectively.

Part 1. We want to show that there is only a small probability that a random sequence of training examples \(S=\langle(x_1,l_1),\ldots,(x_m,l_m)\rangle\), labeled according to \(c\in C\), can cause \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) to generate a hypothesis that is consistent with \(S\) but has error larger than \(\epsilon\).

We first sketch the argument. We consider the following mapping of arbitrary sequences of \(km_0\) labeled examples into hypotheses. The sequence is partitioned into \(k\) blocks of length \(m_0\); each block is fed into WeakLearn, which generates a hypothesis. Finally, these \(k\) hypotheses are combined by a majority vote to generate a single hypothesis. We define two properties on sequences chosen out of \(S\) that are based on the hypothesis to which these sequences are mapped. The first is that the hypothesis is consistent with all the examples in \(S\); the second is that the hypothesis has error larger than \(\epsilon\) with respect to the distribution \(D\) and the underlying concept. We call sequences that have both properties “bad” sequences.

To bound the probability of samples \(S\) from which a bad sequence can be chosen, one can view the elements of \(S\) that are not in the sequence as random test points on which the hypothesis is tested. As most of the points in \(S\) are not in the sequence, it is very unlikely that the hypothesis is consistent with all these examples and yet has a large probability of making an error. This observation, together with the fact that the total number of sequences of \(km_0\) elements from \(S\) is not too large, gives the proof of this part of the theorem.

We now give the formal proof, which is an adaptation of a technique used by Warmuth and Littlestone. Fix any concept \(c\in C\). Let \(S=\langle(x_1,l_1),\ldots,(x_m,l_m)\rangle\) be the sequence of randomly drawn training examples returned by \(EX\) in step 1 of a specific run of \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\), such that for all \(i\), \(l_i=c(x_i)\). Let \(S'=\langle(x_{t_1},l_{t_1}),\ldots,(x_{t_d},l_{t_d})\rangle\) denote a sequence of examples chosen out of \(S\).

Let \(\mathcal T\) be the collection of all \(m^d\) sequences of length \(d=km_0\) of integers in \(\{1,\ldots,m\}\). For any sequence of examples \(S\) and any \(T\in\mathcal T\), denote the selected sequence by \(S'_T\), and denote the hypothesis to which it is mapped by \(h_M(S'_T)\).

Fixing \(T\), let \(U_T\) be the set of all sequences of examples \(S\) such that \(h_M(S'_T)\) has error larger than \(\epsilon\). Let \(C_T\) be the set of all sequences \(S\) such that \(h_M(S'_T)\) is consistent with all the examples in \(S\). If \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) has non-zero probability of generating a consistent hypothesis that has a large error when using the sample \(S\), then there must exist some \(T\in\mathcal T\) such that \(S\in C_T\cap U_T\). We can thus upper bound the probability of failure over the random choice of \(S\) by requiring that

\[\sum_{T\in\mathcal T}\Pr(C_T\cap U_T)\leq\delta/2.\]

The choice of \(h_M(S'_T)\) is only a function of the \(d\) elements of \(S'_T\). If \(S\in U_T\), the hypothesis has probability at least \(\epsilon\) of making a mistake on any of the remaining \(m-d\) elements of \(S\), which are chosen at random independently of the elements in \(S'_T\) and of the fact that \(S\in U_T\). Thus the probability that \(S\) is in \(C_T\), given that it is in \(U_T\), is at most \((1-\epsilon)^{m-d}\). Multiplying by the size of \(\mathcal T\), we get

\[m^d(1-\epsilon)^{m-d}\leq\delta/2.\tag{15}\]

Substituting \(d=km_0\), it is sufficient to require

\[m\geq\frac1\epsilon\left(\ln\frac{2}{\delta}+km_0(\ln m+\epsilon)\right).\]

We now use \((1/(2\gamma^2))\ln(m/2)\) as a choice for \(k\). Corollary 2.3 shows that this choice obeys the inequality in line 2 of \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\). The slightly stronger requirement in the theorem therefore ensures that, if \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) generates a consistent hypothesis, then it has error smaller than \(\epsilon\) with probability at least \(1-\delta/2\).

Part 2. We now bound the probability that \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) generates a hypothesis that is not consistent with the sample. From Theorem 3.1, if all \(k\) hypotheses generated by WeakLearn have error smaller than \(\epsilon_0\) with respect to the corresponding weightings of the sample, then the final hypothesis is consistent with the whole sample.

Each time a hypothesis is returned from WeakLearn, its error on the weighted sample is checked, and it is rejected if the error is too large. Thus the only case in which a hypothesis used by \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) has an error larger than \(\epsilon_0\) is when all the iterations of statement 3(a) fail to generate a hypothesis with small error. As the probability that any single call to WeakLearn generates a good hypothesis is at least \(\lambda\), the probability that all of the \((1/\lambda)\ln(2k/\delta)\) runs of WeakLearn performed in statement 3(a) fail is at most

\[(1-\lambda)^{(1/\lambda)\ln(2k/\delta)}\leq\delta/(2k).\]

Thus the probability that any of the \(k\) hypotheses used is not good is at most \(\delta/2\), completing the proof.

theorems.title: Explicit BSamp sample complexity
theorems.statementHtml:
Let WeakLearn satisfy the assumptions of Theorem 3.2. Then, given any \(\epsilon,\delta>0\), if \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) is required to generate a hypothesis that is consistent with a sample of size

\[m\geq\max\left\{208,\frac2\epsilon\ln\frac2\delta,\frac{16m_0}{\epsilon\gamma^2}\left(\ln\frac{m_0}{\epsilon\gamma^2}\right)^2\right\},\]

then with probability larger than \(1-\delta\), the hypothesis output by \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) has error smaller than \(\epsilon\).
theorems.proofHtml: We want to find \(m\) that will satisfy the inequality in Theorem 3.2. It suffices if \(m\) is larger than the maximum of twice each of the two terms on the right hand side. From the first term we get \(m>(2/\epsilon)\ln(2/\delta)\). To bound \(m\) with respect to the second term, observe that, in general, in order to satisfy \(m>a(\ln m+1)^2\), it suffices to choose \(m=16a(\ln a)^2\), if \(a\geq5\). It thus suffices if \(m>16\cdot5\cdot(\ln5)^2\), or if \(m>208\).

theorems.title: Reliability of the abort condition
theorems.statementHtml: For all \(0\leq i\leq k-1\), the probability, over the distribution of the examples, that an abort is triggered during the generation of \(h_{i+1}\), given that \(t_i\geq2\epsilon(1-\epsilon)/(k\alpha_{\max}^i)\), is smaller than \(\delta/(2k)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(n=\#\mathrm{accept}+\#\mathrm{reject}\) and \(m=\#\mathrm{accept}\). Define \(c=2\epsilon(1-\epsilon)/(k\alpha_{\max}^i)\) and \(n_0=(8/c)\ln(16k/(c\delta))\). An abort occurs after testing the \(n\)th example if and only if \(n>n_0\) and \(m<cn/2\). Let \(q=t_i/\alpha_{\max}^i\) denote the probability that FiltEX accepts a random example generated by \(EX\). We want to prove that if \(q\geq c\), then the probability of an abort during any one of the \(k\) iterations is smaller than \(\delta/(2k)\). Using Chernoff bounds,

\[\Pr(m<cn/2)\leq e^{-cn/8}.\]

Summing this probability over all possible values of \(n\),

\[\Pr(\text{abort after }n>n_0)<\sum_{n=n_0}^{\infty}e^{-cn/8}=\frac{e^{-cn_0/8}}{1-e^{-c/8}}<\frac8c e^{-cn_0/8}<\frac{\delta}{2k},\]

which proves the claim.

theorems.title: Boosting the learner's reliability
theorems.statementHtml:
Assume WeakLearn is a learning algorithm that generates hypotheses whose error is smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\) with probability at least \(\lambda>0\), using \(m_0\) examples. Then, for any \(\delta>0\), Algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Rel}}\) will generate hypotheses whose error is smaller than \(1/2-\gamma/2\) with probability \(1-\delta\). Furthermore, the number of examples required by algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Rel}}\) is at most

\[\frac8{\gamma^2}\left(\ln\ln\frac2\delta+\ln\frac1{\delta\lambda}\right)+\frac{m_0}{\lambda}\ln\frac2\delta.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
The bound on the number of examples is immediate from the definition of the algorithm. To prove that the algorithm is correct, we bound the probability that the resulting hypothesis has error larger than \(1/2-\gamma/2\). There are two events that might cause this. The first is that all of the \(r\) hypotheses generated by WeakLearn have error larger than \(1/2-\gamma\). The second is that a hypothesis that has error larger than \(1/2-\gamma/2\) makes fewer mistakes, on the test sample, than a hypothesis that has error smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\). It is easy to bound the probability of each of those events by \(\delta/2\).

As each call to WeakLearn has probability of at least \(\lambda\) of generating a hypothesis with error smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\), the probability of not generating any accurate enough hypothesis is at most

\[(1-\lambda)^r=(1-\lambda)^{(1/\lambda)\ln(2/\delta)}\leq e^{-\ln(2/\delta)}=\delta/2.\]

For the second event to happen, given that one of the hypotheses has error smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\), there has to be a bad hypothesis whose estimated error is larger than that of the good hypothesis. For this to happen, the gap between the actual error and the estimated error for at least one of the \(r\) hypotheses has to be at least \(\gamma/4\). Using Hoeffding bounds, this probability is at most

\[r e^{-2m(\gamma/4)^2}=r\exp\left[-2\frac8{\gamma^2}\ln\frac{2r}{\delta}\left(\frac\gamma4\right)^2\right]=r e^{-\ln(2r/\delta)}=\delta/2,\]

which proves the lemma.
theorems.proofNote: The proof is deferred to Appendix B.

theorems.title: Correctness of boosting by filtering
theorems.statementHtml: Let WeakLearn be a learning algorithm that, for any distribution over the sample space \(X\) and any \(c\in C\), generates a hypothesis whose error is smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\) with probability \(\lambda\), for some \(\gamma,\lambda>0\). Then, for any \(\delta,\epsilon>0\), the algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\), given \(\lambda,\gamma,\epsilon\), and \(\delta\), generates a hypothesis whose error is smaller than \(\epsilon\) with probability at least \(1-\delta\).
theorems.proofHtml:
From Lemma 3.4 we know that the probability that any of the times the abort condition has been triggered is unjustified is smaller than \(\delta/2\). On the other hand, the properties of Algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Rel}}\), given in Lemma 3.5, guarantee that for each iteration \(0\leq i\leq k-1\), the probability that the error of \(h_i\) is larger than \(1/2-\gamma/2\) is smaller than \(\delta/(2k)\). Combining these claims, we get that with probability at least \(1-\delta\), all the hypotheses have error smaller than \(1/2-\gamma/2\) and all the times the abort condition is triggered are justified. We shall now show that in this case the error of \(h_M\) is smaller than \(\epsilon\).

In applying Lemma 3.7, choose \(\gamma'=\gamma/2\). The probability of any set of examples \(A\) according to the filtered distribution generated by FiltEX is equal to the weight assigned to \(A\) by the weighting scheme defined in Equation (3), with \(\gamma'\) replacing \(\gamma\). Equate the set chosen by the chooser with the set of instances on which \(h_i(x)=c(x)\).

First consider iterations in which the abort condition is not triggered. The hypothesis \(h_i\) is successfully generated and has error smaller than \(1/2-\gamma'\), so \(\widehat\gamma_i>\gamma'\), and Lemma 3.7 implies that the average potential can only decrease.

Next consider the aborted iterations. The error of a random coin flip with respect to any distribution over the examples is one half. Thus \(\widehat\gamma_i=0\), and Lemma 3.7 gives that the average potential increases by at most \(\gamma'\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\alpha_r^i=\gamma t_i/2\). As all aborts are justified, \(t_i<2\epsilon(1-\epsilon)/(k\gamma)\).

Combining the aborted and non-aborted iterations, the total potential increase in all \(k\) iterations is at most \(\epsilon(1-\epsilon)\). Following the same argument as in the proof of Theorem 2.1, and using the choice of \(k\) for which \(\beta_0^0\leq\epsilon^2\), we get

\[\Pr(h_M(x)\neq c(x))=\sum_{r=0}^{k}q_r^k\beta_r^k\leq\beta_0^0+\epsilon(1-\epsilon)\leq\epsilon^2+\epsilon(1-\epsilon)=\epsilon,\]

where the probability is taken with respect to both the random choice of \(x\) according to \(D\), and the random coin flips of the dummy weak hypotheses.

theorems.title: Exact change in average potential
theorems.statementHtml:
Suppose that the weightor in the majority-vote game assigns weights according to \(\gamma'\), and that \(\widehat\gamma_i=\sum_{r=0}^{i}W(M_r^i)\). Then the increase in the average potential, where \(\beta_r^i\) is defined according to \(\gamma'\), is

\[\sum_{r=0}^{i+1}q_r^{i+1}\beta_r^{i+1}=\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\beta_r^i+(\gamma'-\widehat\gamma_i)\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\alpha_r^i.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Recall Equation (9) from the proof of Lemma 2.2:

\[\sum_{r=0}^{i+1}q_r^{i+1}\beta_r^{i+1}=\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\beta_r^i+\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^ix_r^i(\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}-\beta_r^{i+1}).\]

From the definition of \(\widehat\gamma_i\), following the same line of argument as in Equations (10)–(13),

\[\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^ix_r^i(\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}-\beta_r^{i+1})=\left(\frac12+\widehat\gamma_i\right)\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i(\beta_{r+1}^{i+1}-\beta_r^{i+1}).\tag{16}\]

Combining these equations, adding and subtracting the term with \(\gamma'\), and using Equation (5) for the first term and Equation (7) for the second, gives

\[\sum_{r=0}^{i+1}q_r^{i+1}\beta_r^{i+1}=\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\beta_r^i+(\gamma'-\widehat\gamma_i)\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\alpha_r^i,\]

which is the statement of the lemma.

theorems.title: Sample complexity of BFilt
theorems.statementHtml:
The number of training examples required by \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\) is smaller than

\[m=\frac{4\sqrt{2}e^{1/12}}{\sqrt{3\pi}}\frac{k^{3/2}\gamma}{\epsilon(1-\epsilon)}\max\left\{m_R,4\ln\frac{16k^2\gamma}{\delta\epsilon}\right\}<\frac{33}{\epsilon\gamma^2}\left(\ln\frac1\epsilon\right)^{3/2}\max\left\{m_R,12\ln\frac{8\ln(1/\epsilon)}{\gamma\delta\epsilon}\right\},\tag{17}\]

where \(k\) is the number of iterations as chosen in line 1 of \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\), and the inequality is obtained by using the suggested choice of \(k\). The variable \(m_R\) denotes the number of examples for generating a weak hypothesis with reliability \(1-\delta/(2k)\) by \(B_{\mathrm{Rel}}\), and is equal to

\[m_R=\frac{m_0}{\lambda}\ln\frac{4k}{\delta}+\frac8{\gamma^2}\left(\ln\ln\frac{4k}{\delta}+\ln\frac{2k}{\delta\lambda}\right).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
The number of examples required by \(B_{\mathrm{Rel}}\) to generate a hypothesis that has error smaller than \(1/2-\gamma/2\) with probability larger than \(1-\delta/(2k)\), denoted \(m_R\), is easily bounded using Lemma 3.5. The abort condition guarantees that the number of examples tested by FiltEX during iteration \(i\) is at most

\[\frac{k\gamma\alpha_{\max}^i}{\epsilon(1-\epsilon)}\max\left\{m_R,4\ln\frac{16k^2\gamma}{\delta\epsilon}\right\}.\]

Thus the total number of examples is bounded by

\[\max\left\{m_R,4\ln\frac{16k^2\gamma}{\delta\epsilon}\right\}\frac{k\gamma}{\epsilon(1-\epsilon)}\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\alpha_{\max}^i.\tag{18}\]

Using Lemma 3.9 for \(0\leq i\leq k-2\), and observing that \(\alpha_{\max}^{k-1}=1\), we can bound the sum by

\[\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\alpha_{\max}^i\leq\sqrt{\frac{8e^{1/6}}{3\pi}}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\frac1{\sqrt j}+1\right)<\frac{4e^{1/12}}{\sqrt{3\pi}}\sqrt{2k}.\tag{19}\]

The last inequality is true because

\[\sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\frac1{\sqrt j}<1+\int_1^{k-1}\frac{dx}{\sqrt x}=2\sqrt{k-1}-1.\]

Combining (18) and (19) gives the equality in (17), and plugging in the choice \(k=(4/\gamma^2)\ln(1/\epsilon)\) gives the inequality.

theorems.title: Maximum filtering factor
theorems.statementHtml:
For all iterations \(0\leq i\leq k-2\) of \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\),

\[\alpha_{\max}^i\leq\sqrt{\frac{8}{3\pi(k-i-1)}}e^{1/12}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
We can rewrite the definition of \(\alpha_r^i\) from Figure 4 as follows, ignoring the choices of \(r\) that give \(\alpha_r^i=0\) for the purpose of the upper bound:

\[\alpha_r^i=\binom{x}{(1/2-\mu)x}\left(\frac12-\frac\gamma2\right)^{x(1/2-\mu)}\left(\frac12+\frac\gamma2\right)^{x(1/2+\mu)},\]

where \(x=k-i-1\) and \(\mu=1/2-(\lfloor k/2\rfloor-r)/(k-i-1)\). Using the upper bound in Lemma D.1, the last expression, for any value of \(\mu\), is smaller than

\[\sqrt{\frac2{\pi x}}\frac{e^{1/(12x)}}{\sqrt{1-4\mu^2}}\exp\left\{xH\!\left(\frac12-\mu\right)+x\left(\frac12-\mu\right)\ln\left(\frac12-\frac\gamma2\right)+x\left(\frac12+\mu\right)\ln\left(\frac12+\frac\gamma2\right)\right\}.\]

A basic inequality is that, for any \(-1/2\leq\mu\leq1/2\),

\[H\!\left(\frac12-\mu\right)\leq-\left(\frac12-\mu\right)\ln\left(\frac12-\frac\gamma2\right)-\left(\frac12+\mu\right)\ln\left(\frac12+\frac\gamma2\right).\]

This inequality is strict unless \(\mu=\gamma/2\). From this we get

\[\alpha_r^i<\sqrt{\frac2{\pi x}}\frac{e^{1/(12x)}}{\sqrt{1-\gamma^2}}.\]

As \(\gamma\leq1/2\) and \(x\geq1\), we get the statement of the lemma.
theorems.proofNote: The paper says the proof is in Appendix D. The appendix heading reads “Proof of Lemma 3.10”, but the proof itself is explicitly labeled “Proof of Lemma 3.9”; the heading is a typographical error in the supplied PDF.

theorems.title: Randomized BSamp sample complexity
theorems.section: 3.4 Randomized learning algorithms and randomized hypotheses
theorems.statementHtml:
Let WeakLearn be a randomized learning algorithm that generates, with probability \(\lambda>0\) over its internal randomization and the random choice of the training examples, a randomized hypothesis whose error is smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\), for some \(\gamma>0\). Assume the number of training examples required to achieve this is uniformly bounded by \(m_0\). Suppose that \(m\), the size of the sample used by \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\), obeys

\[m\geq\max\left\{208,\frac2\epsilon\ln\frac2\delta,16a(\ln a)^2\right\},\qquad a=\frac{m_0+\ln(1/\lambda)+\ln\ln(1/(\gamma^2\delta))}{\epsilon\gamma^2}.\tag{20}\]

Then with probability at least \(1-\delta\), the hypothesis \(h_M\) generated by \(B_{\mathrm{Samp}}\) has error smaller than \(\epsilon\).
theorems.proofHtml:
The essential difference from the proof of Theorem 3.2 is that the number of possible hypotheses that can be generated from the sample is larger. In Theorem 3.2 this number is the number of subsequences of size \(d\) that can be chosen from a sequence of size \(m\), namely \(m^d\). Here it is the number of subsequences times the number of combinations of values of the parameter \(q\) that could have been used in generating the \(k\) good hypotheses. Assume that \(q=ir+l\), where \(i=0,\ldots,k-1\) is the number of hypotheses generated so far, \(l=1,\ldots,r\) is the counter of attempts to generate a good \(i\)th hypothesis, and \(r=(1/\lambda)\ln(2k/\delta)\). Each hypothesis can be chosen using one of \(r\) values, and the total number of combinations is \(r^k\). Thus the basic inequality replacing (15) is

\[r^km^d(1-\epsilon)^{m-d}<\delta/2.\tag{21}\]

Solving for \(m\), it is enough that

\[m\geq\frac1\epsilon\left(\ln\frac2\delta+\frac{m_0}{2\gamma^2}(\ln m+1)^2+\frac{\ln m}{2\gamma^2}\left(\ln\frac1\lambda+\ln\ln\frac2\delta+\ln\ln\ln m\right)\right).\]

Using the same argument used to prove Corollary 3.3 gives the statement of the theorem.

theorems.title: Resource bounds for polynomial PAC learning
theorems.section: 3.5 The resources needed for polynomial PAC learning
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(C\) is a weakly PAC learnable concept class, parameterized by \(n\) and \(s\) in the standard way, then there exists a PAC learning algorithm for \(C\) that learns with accuracy \(\epsilon\) and reliability \(\delta\) and:

  • requires a sample of size \((1/\epsilon)(\log(1/\epsilon))^{3/2}(\log\log(1/\epsilon)+\log(1/\delta))p_1(n,s)\),
  • halts in time \((1/\epsilon)(\log(1/\epsilon))^{5/2}(\log\log(1/\epsilon)+\log(1/\delta))p_2(n,s)\),
  • uses space \((\log(1/\epsilon))(\log\log(1/\epsilon)+\log(1/\delta))p_3(n,s)\), and
  • outputs hypotheses of size \((\log(1/\epsilon))p_4(n,s)\), evaluatable in time \((\log(1/\epsilon))p_5(n,s)\),

for some polynomials \(p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4\), and \(p_5\).
theorems.proofHtml: The PAC learning algorithm referred to is algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\) applied to the given weak PAC learning algorithm. As \(n\) and \(s\) are passed without change to the weak learning algorithm, the dependence on \(n\) and \(s\) remains polynomial. Fixing \(\lambda\) and \(\gamma\), Theorem 3.8 gives the dependence of the sample complexity on \(\epsilon\) and \(\delta\). The time and space complexity of the boosted algorithm, as well as the size of the final hypothesis, follow from the discussion of the resources required by \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\) which follows Theorem 3.8.

theorems.title: Lower bound on the number of weak hypotheses
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(\epsilon,\gamma\), and \(\delta\) be positive real numbers and let \(k\) be an integer such that

\[\sum_{i=\lfloor k/2\rfloor+1}^{k}\binom{k}{i}\left(\frac12-\gamma\right)^i\left(\frac12+\gamma\right)^{k-i}>\epsilon.\]

Let \(B\) be a general boosting algorithm. Then there exists a concept class \(C\), a concept \(c\in C\), and a weak learning algorithm WeakLearn for \(C\) such that the following holds with probability at least \(1-\delta\) over the random choice of the instances and the internal randomization of WeakLearn:

  • WeakLearn returns hypotheses whose errors are at most \(1/2-\gamma\) with respect to the corresponding distributions.
  • If \(B\) calls WeakLearn at most \(k\) times, then the error of the hypothesis generated by \(B\) is at least \(\epsilon\).
theorems.proofHtml:
We show that for a sufficiently large \(N\), one of the concept classes in \(F_N\) satisfies the statement. Suppose that \(C_r\in F_N\) is chosen randomly by selecting \(r\) uniformly from \(\{0,1\}^N\). After this selection, \(c\in C_r\) is chosen randomly with equal odds to be either \(c_r^0\) or \(c_r^1\). We shall show that the expected error of any boosting algorithm, with respect to this distribution over the target concepts, is larger than \(\epsilon\).

As each concept class includes only two concepts which disagree on each point of the domain, the weak learner can identify the concept after observing any single example. We construct the hypotheses so that the boosting algorithm can gain as little information as possible without violating the requirement on their performance. The weak learner is not limited in computational power and can approximate \(\Pr_{D_i}(x)\) to any desired accuracy for all \(x\in X\).

Given \(D_i,\gamma\), and \(N\), the weak learner selects \(h_i\) by choosing the set \(A_i\), on which \(h_i(x)=c(x)\), as follows. First, \(A_i'\) contains all elements \(x\in X\) for which \(\Pr_{D_i}(x)\geq N^{-2/3}\). On the rest of the space, each element in \(X-A_i'\) is added independently to \(A_i''\) with probability \(1/2+\gamma+N^{-1/12}\). Write \(\gamma'=\gamma+N^{-1/12}\). Set \(A_i=A_i'\cup A_i''\), and let \(h_i\) be \(c(x)\) if \(x\in A_i\), and \(1-c(x)\) otherwise.

We need to show that for sufficiently large \(N\), this procedure generates a hypothesis whose error under \(D_i\) is smaller than \(1/2-\gamma\) with high probability. As \(h_i(x)=c(x)\) for all \(x\in A_i'\), it suffices to show that the weight of \(A_i''\) is at least \(1/2+\gamma\) of the weight of \(X-A_i'\). Its expected weight is \((1/2+\gamma')\Pr_{D_i}(X-A_i')\). At most \(N^{2/3}\) elements can have sufficient weight to be included in \(A_i'\). Thus there are between \(N-N^{2/3}\) and \(N\) elements in \(X-A_i'\), each of weight at most \(N^{-2/3}\). Thus the variance of the weight of \(A_i''\) is at most \(N^{1/2}N^{-2/3}=N^{-1/6}=o(N^{-1/12})\). Since \(\gamma'-\gamma=N^{-1/12}\), standard bounds on deviations of large sums show that the weight of \(A_i''\) is larger than \((1/2+\gamma)\Pr_{D_i}(X-A_i')\) with probability approaching 1 as \(N\to\infty\).

We now lower bound the error of the prediction of \(c(x)\). Construct the Bayes optimal rule for this weak learner. In the first case, one of the \(k\) distributions assigns \(x\) weight larger than \(N^{-2/3}\); the corresponding hypothesis gives the correct label. The total probability of such points is at most \(kN^{-1/3}\), which approaches zero. In the second case, \(x\) is an instance whose label has been observed in one of the examples accessed by \(B\). Denote this set by \(M\). The probability of this event is at most \(|M|/N\), which also approaches zero. In the third case, which amounts to most of the probability, each of the \(k\) hypotheses is chosen independently to be \(c(x)\) with probability \(1/2+\gamma'\), and \(1-c(x)\) with probability \(1/2-\gamma'\).

Let \(n(x)\) be the number of hypotheses such that \(h_i(x)=1\), and set \(Z=X-M-\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}A_i'\). Bayes’ formula gives

\[\Pr(c(x)=1\mid x\in Z,n(x)=n)=\frac{\frac12(\frac12+\gamma')^{n(x)}(\frac12-\gamma')^{k-n(x)}}{\frac12(\frac12+\gamma')^{n(x)}(\frac12-\gamma')^{k-n(x)}+\frac12(\frac12-\gamma')^{n(x)}(\frac12+\gamma')^{k-n(x)}}.\tag{23}\]

This value is larger than \(1/2\) if and only if \(n(x)>k/2\). Thus the Bayes optimal decision rule when \(x\in Z\) is to predict according to the majority of the weak hypotheses. Its probability of error is lower bounded by

\[\begin{aligned}&\Pr_D(Z)\Pr(\operatorname{MAJ}(h_1(x),\ldots,h_k(x))\neq c(x))\\&\quad\geq(1-|M|/N-kN^{-1/3})\sum_{i=\lfloor k/2\rfloor+1}^{k}\binom{k}{i}(1/2-\gamma-N^{-1/12})^i(1/2+\gamma+N^{-1/12})^{k-i}\\&\quad\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}\sum_{i=\lfloor k/2\rfloor+1}^{k}\binom{k}{i}(1/2-\gamma)^i(1/2+\gamma)^{k-i}.\end{aligned}\tag{24}\]

As this is a lower bound on the error of the Bayes rule, it is a lower bound on the expected error of any hypothesis generated by a boosting algorithm. Hence there exists a choice of concept class \(C_r\) and concept \(c\in C_r\) for which the error is at least as large as (24). Together with the assumption on \(k\), this completes the proof.
theorems.proofNote: The proof occupies the remainder of Section 3.6 under the printed heading “Proof of the theorem”.

theorems.title: Relaxed accuracy requirements
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(0<\gamma,\epsilon\leq1/2\) be the accuracy parameters supplied to \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\), and \(k\) be the number of iterations chosen by \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\). Let \(t_i\) denote \(\sum_{r=0}^{i}q_r^i\alpha_r^i\), and let \(1/2-\widehat\gamma_i\) denote the error of \(h_i\) with respect to the filtered distribution in the \(i\)th iteration. If, for each iteration \(0\leq i\leq k-1\),

\[\widehat\gamma_i\geq\gamma\left(1-\frac{\epsilon(1-\epsilon)}{t_i k\gamma}\right),\tag{25}\]

then the error of \(h_M\), the hypothesis output by \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\), with respect to the target distribution, is smaller than \(\epsilon\).
theorems.proofHtml: From Lemma 3.7 we immediately get that the increase of the average potential in each iteration is at most \(\epsilon(1-\epsilon)/k\). Thus the total increase in the average potential in all \(k\) iterations is \(\epsilon(1-\epsilon)\). The rest of the proof follows the same line of argument as the one used for the aborted iterations in the proof of Theorem 3.6.

theorems.title: Maximal-ratio divergence dominates KL divergence
theorems.statementHtml:
For any two distributions \(Q\) and \(P\), defined on the same measure space,

\[D_M(Q\mathbin\Vert P)\geq D_{KL}(Q\mathbin\Vert P),\]

where \(D_{KL}(Q\mathbin\Vert P)\) is the Kullback-Leibler divergence, which is defined as

\[D_{KL}(Q\mathbin\Vert P):=\mathbb E_{x\sim Q}\ln\frac{Q(x)}{P(x)}.\]
theorems.proofHtml: If \(\mathbb E_Q\ln(Q(x)/P(x))\geq a\), then there exists a set \(A\) such that \(Q(A)>0\) and \(\ln(Q(A)/P(A))\geq a\), which implies that \(D_M(Q\mathbin\Vert P)\geq a\).

theorems.title: Acceptance rate and distribution divergence
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(D\) is the target distribution, and \(F_i\) is the distribution generated by FiltEX during the \(i\)th iteration, then

\[t_i\leq e^{-D_M(F_i\Vert D)}\leq e^{-D_{KL}(F_i\Vert D)}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
The second inequality follows from Lemma 4.2. To prove the first inequality, assume that \(D_M(F_i\Vert D)\geq a\). Then there exists a set \(A\in\Sigma\) such that \(F_i(A)/D(A)\geq e^a\). Using the definition of the measure generated by filtering in Equation (3),

\[e^a\leq\frac{\sum_{r=0}^{i}D(A\cap X_r^i)\alpha_r^i/Z_i}{\sum_{r=0}^{i}D(A\cap X_r^i)}\leq\frac{\sum_{r=0}^{i}D(A\cap X_r^i)/Z_i}{\sum_{r=0}^{i}D(A\cap X_r^i)}=\frac1{Z_i}.\]

The inequality holds because \(\alpha_r^i\leq1\) always. Here \(Z_i=\sum_{r=0}^{i}D(X_r^i)\alpha_r^i=t_i\), from which \(1/t_i\geq e^a\), proving the lemma.

theorems.title: Boosting a distribution-specific learner
theorems.statementHtml:
Fix a target distribution \(D\) and real-valued parameters \(\gamma,\epsilon,\delta>0\). If WeakLearn is a learning algorithm that, for any distribution \(P\) over the sample space \(X\) and any \(c\in C\), generates a hypothesis whose error, with respect to \(P\), is smaller than

\[\frac12-\gamma\left(1-\frac{\epsilon(1-\epsilon)e^{D_{KL}(P\Vert D)}}{4\ln(1/\epsilon)}\right),\]

then, with probability at least \(1-\delta\), the algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\), given the parameters, generates a hypothesis whose error, with respect to \(D\), is smaller than \(\epsilon\).
theorems.proofHtml:
The algorithm uses \(k=(4/\gamma^2)\ln(1/\epsilon)\), as given in statement 1 of Algorithm \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\). From Lemma 4.1, it is enough if the error in the \(i\)th iteration is smaller than

\[\frac12-\gamma\left(1-\frac{\epsilon(1-\epsilon)}{4t_i\ln(1/\epsilon)}\right).\]

Combining Lemmas 4.3 and 4.2, we get that \(t_i\leq e^{-D_{KL}(F_i\Vert D)}\), which proves the theorem.

theorems.title: Parallel PAC learning
theorems.statementHtml: If \(C\) is a polynomially PAC-learnable concept class, then there exists a parallel learning algorithm for \(C\) that runs on a PRAM machine with \(O(1/\epsilon)\) processors whose time-complexity dependence on the accuracy is \(O(\log(1/\epsilon))\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Observe that the time complexity of \(B_{\mathrm{Filt}}\) is dominated by the time spent by FiltEX checking examples that are eventually rejected. The probability that any given example is accepted during generation of the \(i\)th hypothesis is constant, independent of whether any other example is tested or accepted during that stage.

On a PRAM with \(p\) processors, run FiltEX independently on each processor, each making separate calls to \(EX\), so that they test different random examples. When one processor accepts an example, halt all the other processors and ignore their results. The accepted example is returned to WeakLearn.

Out of the \(O((1/\epsilon)(\ln(1/\epsilon))^{3/2})\) examples needed for learning, only \(O(\ln(1/\epsilon))\) examples have to be accepted and returned to WeakLearn. If \(p=O((1/\epsilon)\sqrt{\ln(1/\epsilon)})\), then the search for an acceptable example takes expected constant time, so the expected running time becomes \(O(\ln(1/\epsilon))\). If \(p\) is smaller, a \(p\)-fold speedup over serial execution is achieved.
theorems.proofNote: The argument immediately preceding the theorem is its proof; no separate proof follows the displayed theorem statement.

theorems.title: Stirling bounds for generalized binomial coefficients
theorems.statementHtml:
For any real numbers \(x\geq1\) and \(0\leq\mu<1/2\),

\[\exp\left(-\frac{1}{3(1-4\mu^2)x}\right)<\sqrt{\frac{\pi x}{2}}\binom{x}{x(1/2-\mu)}e^{-xH(1/2-\mu)}<\frac{\exp(1/(12x))}{\sqrt{1-4\mu^2}},\tag{28}\]

where \(H(y)=-y\ln y-(1-y)\ln(1-y)\) is the entropy function, and the extension of the binomial function to the reals is based on the extension of the factorial to the Gamma function, \(x!=\Gamma(x+1)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
The proof is based on the Stirling approximation. Notice that as \(x\to\infty\), the lower bound converges to 1 while the upper bound converges to \((1-4\mu^2)^{-1/2}\). In other words, for large values of \(x\), the binomial coefficient \(\binom{x}{x(1/2-\mu)}\) is related to the exponential function in the denominator by a small factor.

Stirling approximation to the factorial can be written as

\[x\ln x-x+\frac12\ln x+\ln\sqrt{2\pi}<\ln(x!)<x\ln x-x+\frac12\ln x+\ln\sqrt{2\pi}+\frac1{12x}.\]

For the lower bound, applying the lower estimate to \(x!\) and the upper estimate to the two denominator factorials gives

\[\begin{aligned}\ln\binom{x}{x(1/2-\mu)}&>xH(1/2-\mu)-\ln\sqrt{2\pi x}+\ln2-\frac{1}{3(1-4\mu^2)x}.\end{aligned}\]

For the upper bound, applying the upper estimate to \(x!\) and the lower estimate to the denominator factorials gives

\[\ln\binom{x}{x(1/2-\mu)}<xH(1/2-\mu)-\ln\sqrt{2\pi x}+\ln2-\frac12\ln(1-4\mu^2)+\frac1{12x}.\]

Exponentiating these two inequalities yields (28).
theorems.proofNote: The appendix heading says “Proof of Lemma 3.10” in the supplied PDF, although the appendix actually proves Lemma D.1 and then Lemma 3.9. This heading is preserved as the section label and documented as a paper typo.
